subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: f(x) = √(25-x²) — Derivative, Tangent Line, Continuity of g & Substitution Integral
stimulus: The function $f$ is defined by $f(x) = \sqrt{25 - x^2}$ for $-5 \le x \le 5$.

parts.prompt: Write an equation for the line tangent to the graph of $f$ at $x = -3$.

Let $g$ be the function defined by $g(x) = \begin{cases} f(x) & \text{for } -5 \le x \le -3 \\ x + 7 & \text{for } -3 < x \le 5 \end{cases}$. Is $g$ continuous at $x = -3$? Use the definition of continuity to explain your answer.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Considers both one-sided limits: $\lim_{x \to -3^-} g(x) = \lim_{x \to -3^-} f(x) = 4$ and $\lim_{x \to -3^+} g(x) = \lim_{x \to -3^+} (x+7) = 4$
1 pt: Correct conclusion with justification: $\lim_{x \to -3} g(x) = 4 = g(-3)$, so $g$ is continuous at $x = -3$
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Both one-sided limits equal 4, and $g(-3) = f(-3) = 4$. Since $\lim_{x \to -3} g(x) = g(-3)$, $g$ is continuous at $x = -3$.

parts.prompt: Find the value of $\int_0^5 x\sqrt{25-x^2}\, dx$.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Uses substitution: let $u = 25 - x^2$, $du = -2x\, dx$; limits change from $u = 25$ (at $x=0$) to $u = 0$ (at $x=5$)
1 pt: Correct antiderivative: $\int x\sqrt{25-x^2}\, dx = -\frac{1}{2}\int \sqrt{u}\, du = -\frac{1}{3}u^{3/2}$
